Twin Cities Rental Price Analysis

Ha Phan

2026-05-27

Load Libraries

library(DBI)
library(RSQLite)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(corrplot)
library(psych)
library(knitr)

Connect to Database

conn <- dbConnect(SQLite(), 
  "/Users/crystalphan/Desktop/Apartent rental project/twincities_rental.db")

df <- dbGetQuery(conn, "SELECT * FROM annual_summary")
cat("Rows loaded:", nrow(df), "\n")
## Rows loaded: 489
cat("Columns:", ncol(df), "\n")
## Columns: 14

Preview Data

head(df)
##   regionname        city         countyname state year avg_rent min_rent
## 1      54016      Hudson Saint Croix County    WI 2023  1586.62  1518.73
## 2      54016      Hudson Saint Croix County    WI 2024  1660.06  1624.74
## 3      54016      Hudson Saint Croix County    WI 2025  1737.91  1703.48
## 4      54016      Hudson Saint Croix County    WI 2026  1825.30  1797.34
## 5      54022 River Falls      Pierce County    WI 2024  1596.59  1573.96
## 6      54022 River Falls      Pierce County    WI 2025  1655.53  1617.96
##   max_rent months_of_data rent_studio rent_1br rent_2br rent_3br rent_4br
## 1  1634.42             11        1240     1410     1710     2260     2530
## 2  1688.41             12        1240     1410     1710     2260     2530
## 3  1768.33             12        1240     1410     1710     2260     2530
## 4  1849.04              4        1240     1410     1710     2260     2530
## 5  1617.95             12        1100     1250     1520     2020     2270
## 6  1707.47             12        1100     1250     1520     2020     2270

Descriptive Statistics

describe(df[, c("avg_rent", "rent_studio", "rent_1br", 
                "rent_2br", "rent_3br", "rent_4br")])
##             vars   n    mean     sd  median trimmed    mad     min     max
## avg_rent       1 489 1547.24 272.82 1506.64 1532.94 255.93  755.12 2585.29
## rent_studio    2 489 1333.11 182.31 1290.00 1317.25 192.74 1100.00 1860.00
## rent_1br       3 489 1508.22 205.00 1460.00 1490.03 207.56 1250.00 2100.00
## rent_2br       4 489 1834.58 250.16 1780.00 1812.24 266.87 1520.00 2560.00
## rent_3br       5 489 2428.49 330.65 2360.00 2398.40 341.00 2020.00 3390.00
## rent_4br       6 489 2718.71 368.20 2640.00 2684.02 385.48 2270.00 3790.00
##               range skew kurtosis    se
## avg_rent    1830.17 0.53     0.75 12.34
## rent_studio  760.00 0.78     0.06  8.24
## rent_1br     850.00 0.79     0.03  9.27
## rent_2br    1040.00 0.79     0.06 11.31
## rent_3br    1370.00 0.80     0.07 14.95
## rent_4br    1520.00 0.81     0.08 16.65

Chart 1: Average Rent by Year (Twin Cities Trend)

# Calculate average rent per year across all zip codes
yearly <- df %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  summarise(avg_rent = mean(avg_rent))

# Plot the trend line
ggplot(yearly, aes(x = year, y = avg_rent)) +
  geom_line(color = "#185FA5", size = 1.2) +
  geom_point(color = "#185FA5", size = 3) +
  labs(
    title = "Average Rent Trend — Twin Cities (2015–2026)",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Average Rent ($)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
## Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0.
## ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead.
## This warning is displayed once per session.
## Call `lifecycle::last_lifecycle_warnings()` to see where this warning was
## generated.

Chart 2: Rent Distribution by Zip Code (2025)

# Filter to 2025 only
df_2025 <- df %>% filter(year == 2025)

# Histogram of rent distribution
ggplot(df_2025, aes(x = avg_rent)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20, fill = "#185FA5", color = "white") +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Average Rent by Zip Code (2025)",
    x = "Average Rent ($)",
    y = "Number of Zip Codes"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Chart 3: Top 10 Most Expensive vs Affordable Zip Codes (2025)

# Get top 5 most expensive and top 5 most affordable
top5    <- df_2025 %>% arrange(desc(avg_rent)) %>% head(5)
bottom5 <- df_2025 %>% arrange(avg_rent) %>% head(5)
combined <- rbind(top5, bottom5)

ggplot(combined, aes(x = reorder(regionname, avg_rent), 
                      y = avg_rent, fill = avg_rent)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity") +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#B5D4F4", high = "#0C447C") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Most Expensive vs Most Affordable Zip Codes (2025)",
    x = "Zip Code",
    y = "Average Rent ($)",
    fill = "Rent ($)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Chart 4: Rent by Bedroom Size (HUD Fair Market Rents)

# Average HUD rent by bedroom size across all zip codes
bedroom_avg <- df %>%
  summarise(
    Studio   = mean(rent_studio),
    `1 BR`   = mean(rent_1br),
    `2 BR`   = mean(rent_2br),
    `3 BR`   = mean(rent_3br),
    `4 BR`   = mean(rent_4br)
  ) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_longer(everything(), 
                       names_to = "bedroom", 
                       values_to = "avg_rent")

ggplot(bedroom_avg, aes(x = bedroom, y = avg_rent, fill = bedroom)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity") +
  labs(
    title = "Average Fair Market Rent by Bedroom Size — Minneapolis MSA",
    x = "Bedroom Size",
    y = "Average Rent ($)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

Correlation Matrix

# Select numeric columns for correlation
cor_data <- df[, c("avg_rent", "rent_studio", "rent_1br", 
                    "rent_2br", "rent_3br", "rent_4br")]

# Calculate correlation matrix
cor_matrix <- cor(cor_data, use = "complete.obs")

# Plot it
corrplot(cor_matrix, 
         method = "color",
         type = "upper",
         tl.col = "black",
         tl.srt = 45,
         addCoef.col = "black",
         number.cex = 0.7,
         title = "Correlation Matrix — Rent Variables",
         mar = c(0,0,1,0))

Close Database Connection

dbDisconnect(conn)
cat("Database connection closed.")
## Database connection closed.

Regression Model: What Predicts Rent in the Twin Cities?

# We want to answer: which factors predict higher rent?
# Our model: avg_rent ~ year + bedroom sizes

# First filter to complete rows only (no missing values)
df_model <- df %>%
  filter(!is.na(avg_rent) & !is.na(rent_1br))

cat("Rows used in model:", nrow(df_model), "\n")
## Rows used in model: 489

Build the Regression Model

# lm() = linear model
# avg_rent is what we are predicting (dependent variable)
# year, rent_1br, rent_2br, rent_3br are our predictors (independent variables)

model <- lm(avg_rent ~ year + rent_1br + rent_2br + rent_3br, 
            data = df_model)

# Show the full results
summary(model)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = avg_rent ~ year + rent_1br + rent_2br + rent_3br, 
##     data = df_model)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -352.84  -95.94   -8.10   64.67  761.13 
## 
## Coefficients:
##               Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept) -1.094e+05  5.844e+03 -18.717  < 2e-16 ***
## year         5.417e+01  2.888e+00  18.754  < 2e-16 ***
## rent_1br     1.471e+00  2.514e+00   0.585  0.55872    
## rent_2br     1.068e+01  3.499e+00   3.051  0.00241 ** 
## rent_3br    -8.424e+00  1.968e+00  -4.280 2.25e-05 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 151.2 on 484 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.6954, Adjusted R-squared:  0.6928 
## F-statistic: 276.2 on 4 and 484 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

Interpret the Results

# Extract key numbers from the model
r_squared  <- summary(model)$r.squared
adj_r2     <- summary(model)$adj.r.squared
f_stat     <- summary(model)$fstatistic[1]

cat("R-squared:         ", round(r_squared, 4), "\n")
## R-squared:          0.6954
cat("Adjusted R-squared:", round(adj_r2, 4), "\n")
## Adjusted R-squared: 0.6928
cat("F-statistic:       ", round(f_stat, 2), "\n")
## F-statistic:        276.2
cat("\n")
cat("What this means:\n")
## What this means:
cat("  R-squared close to 1.0 = model explains rent variation well\n")
##   R-squared close to 1.0 = model explains rent variation well
cat("  R-squared close to 0.0 = model explains very little\n")
##   R-squared close to 0.0 = model explains very little

Check Model Assumptions

# These 4 plots check if our regression assumptions are met
# This is the same check you did in your MPG project!
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))   # arrange 4 plots in a 2x2 grid
plot(model)

par(mfrow = c(1, 1))   # reset back to normal

Predicted vs Actual Rent

# Add predicted values to our data
df_model$predicted_rent <- predict(model)

# Plot predicted vs actual
ggplot(df_model, aes(x = predicted_rent, y = avg_rent)) +
  geom_point(color = "#185FA5", alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_abline(intercept = 0, slope = 1, 
              color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "Predicted vs Actual Rent",
    subtitle = "Points close to the red line = good predictions",
    x = "Predicted Rent ($)",
    y = "Actual Rent ($)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Key Findings

# Show the coefficients in a clean table
coef_table <- as.data.frame(summary(model)$coefficients)
coef_table <- round(coef_table, 4)
kable(coef_table, 
      caption = "Regression Coefficients",
      col.names = c("Estimate", "Std Error", "t Value", "p Value"))
Regression Coefficients
Estimate Std Error t Value p Value
(Intercept) -109387.2523 5844.2159 -18.7172 0.0000
year 54.1691 2.8884 18.7541 0.0000
rent_1br 1.4712 2.5142 0.5852 0.5587
rent_2br 10.6752 3.4990 3.0509 0.0024
rent_3br -8.4237 1.9679 -4.2805 0.0000
